coredata.dc:title: Visible light activated time dependent phosphorescence colors from carbon dots based materials
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Mei
Sun, Xiaobo
Pan, Wei
Wang, Xiuxia
Wang, Jinping
coredata.dc:description: To endow carbon dots (CDs)-based materials with time dependent phosphorescence colors (TDPCs) activated by visible light will benefit high-level data encryption and anticounterfeiting. It needs multiple emission centers of triplet state stabilized by noncovalent bonds. In this work, levofloxacin and ethanolamine were applied to synthesize CDs (LEE-CDs), in which, aromatic moieties and multiple emissive centers were formed by the reaction between fluorinated quinolone moieties and ethanolamine. Melamine (MA) and cyanuric acid (CA) with large π systems were selected as matrices, and urea and biuret without π systems were selected as the comparison matrices. Interestingly, LEE-CDs encapsulated in MA (LEE-CDs@MA) exhibited visible-light-activated TDPCs varying from red to yellow green, while LEE-CDs@CA displayed TDPCs from red to yellow green after excited by visible light, yellow to green after excited by UV light. However, LEE-CDs@Urea and LEE-CDs@Biuret could only generate a single color RTP after excited by visible or UV light. It can be inferred that π-π stacking interactions combining with hydrogen bonds activated the O-related triplet state with a short lifetime, and hydrogen bonds only retained the N-related triplet state in LEE-CDs with a long lifetime and the emissive center of matrices with an intermediate lifetime. Therefore, the dynamic ratio of the RTP emissive bands from the three centers changing over time resulted in TDPCs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Time dependent phosphorescence colors
Anticounterfeiting
π-π stacking interaction
Visible light

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation and purification processes of LEE-CDs, LEE-CDs@MA, LEE-CDs@CA, LEE-CDs@Urea, LEE-CDs@Biuret

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) The TEM image and the size distribution histogram of LEE-CDs. (B) The FTIR spectrum of LEE-CDs. (C) The absorption spectrum of LEE-CDs. (D) The fluorescence emission spectra of LEE-CDs at diﬀerent excitation wavelengths.
The TEM images and the size distribution histograms of (A) LEE-CDs@Urea, (B) LEE-CDs@Biuret, (C) LEE-CDs@MA and (D) LEE-CDs@CA.

(A) The photographs of LEE-CDs@Urea and LEE-CDs@Biuret powders after turning off the visible and 365 nm UV lights. The RTP emission spectra of (B) LEE-CDs@Urea and (C) LEE-CDs@Biuret under excitation at diﬀerent wavelengths. The Time-resolved phosphorescence emission spectra of LEE-CDs@Urea and LEE-CDs@Biuret after excited at 400 nm (D, E) and 360 nm (F, G).

The photographs of (A) LEE-CDs@MA and (B) LEE-CDs@CA powders after turning off the visible and 365 nm UV lights. The RTP emission spectra of (C) LEE-CDs@MA and (D) LEE-CDs@CA under excitation at different wavelengths.
The RTP emission spectra of LEE-CDs@MA after excitation at 400 nm and three emission peaks ﬁtted by Gaussian at (A) 10 ms, (B) 89 ms, (C) 405 ms, and (D) 721 ms.
The RTP emission spectra of LEE-CDs@CA after excitation at 400 nm and three emission peaks ﬁtted by Gaussian at (A) 10 ms, (B) 200 ms, (C) 500 ms and (D) 900 ms.
The schematic illustration for the mechanisms of TDPCS.
The time-dependent RTP color photographs of butterfly and rabbit patterns, achieved by coating LEE-CDs@MA composite on the papers, after switching off visible or 365 nm UV lamps.

head.ce:title: Visible light activated time dependent phosphorescence colors from carbon dots based materials

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemical and Pharmaceutical Sciences, Qingdao Agricultural University, 700 Changcheng Road, Qingdao, 266109, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemical and Pharmaceutical Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemical and Pharmaceutical Sciences, Qingdao Agricultural University, 700 Changcheng Road, Qingdao, 266109, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: To endow carbon dots (CDs)-based materials with time dependent phosphorescence colors (TDPCs) activated by visible light will benefit high-level data encryption and anticounterfeiting. It needs multiple emission centers of triplet state stabilized by noncovalent bonds. In this work, levofloxacin and ethanolamine were applied to synthesize CDs (LEE-CDs), in which, aromatic moieties and multiple emissive centers were formed by the reaction between fluorinated quinolone moieties and ethanolamine. Melamine (MA) and cyanuric acid (CA) with large π systems were selected as matrices, and urea and biuret without π systems were selected as the comparison matrices. Interestingly, LEE-CDs encapsulated in MA (LEE-CDs@MA) exhibited visible-light-activated TDPCs varying from red to yellow green, while LEE-CDs@CA displayed TDPCs from red to yellow green after excited by visible light, yellow to green after excited by UV light. However, LEE-CDs@Urea and LEE-CDs@Biuret could only generate a single color RTP after excited by visible or UV light. It can be inferred that π-π stacking interactions combining with hydrogen bonds activated the O-related triplet state with a short lifetime, and hydrogen bonds only retained the N-related triplet state in LEE-CDs with a long lifetime and the emissive center of matrices with an intermediate lifetime. Therefore, the dynamic ratio of the RTP emissive bands from the three centers changing over time resulted in TDPCs.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Room temperature phosphorescence (RTP) materials have attracted extensive attention in the fields of information storage [1], data encryption [2], anticounterfeiting [3] and bioimaging [4], because of their long lifetime. Compared to inorganic and organic RTP materials, carbon dots (CDs)-based RTP materials have shown many advantages, such as tunable optical properties [5], high photostability [6], low cytotoxicity [7], as well as, facile preparation with rich source and simple methods [8,9]. However, a single emission color with the intensity changing over time is still the limitation for most of the reported CDs-based RTP materials in practical applications, since they can only accomplish single-tunnel information storage [10], data encryption [11], anticounterfeiting [12], and bioimaging with low sensitivity and accuracy [13]. Moreover, time dependent phosphorescence colors (TDPCs) are featured with the dynamic ratio of the RTP emissive bands changing over time, which can realize multilevel modes of information storage, data encryption, anticounterfeiting and bioimaging by adding the time dimension [14–16]. Given their own advantages, the CDs-based RTP materials endowed with TDPCs will arouse much more interests in improving the security level and bioimaging sensitivity and accuracy.

In general, different color RTP emissions from multiple distinguishable centers with different lifetimes excited by the same wavelength light are the key elements for the CDs-based materials to produce TDPCs. From this viewpoint, it would naturally consider mixing two or more chromophores with different color and lifetime RTP emissions to obtain such TDPCs materials. For example, Qu et al. [17] combined two independent RTP emissive centers from CDs and matrices respectively to produce TDPCs with RTP colors changing from orange to green over time. Shi et al. [18] creatively applied the two structural effects of NaCl matrix to trigger TDPCs performance of CDs which possessed only one single emission center. In this composite, the structural confinement activated the green phosphorescence of the CDs, while, the structural defect produced by metal doping activated the tunneling-related yellow phosphorescence. Interestingly, Tan et al. [19] printed CDs on a piece of paper to generate TDPCs varying from orange to green after excited by the light at a suitable wavelength, in which, the oxide-related and the N-related triplet states of the CDs were individually activated to generate red and green RTPs. Li et al. [20] also printed CDs on a piece of paper to construct TDPCs system with color changing from yellow green to blue, resulting from surface state-related blue RTP and the carbon core state-related yellow-green RTP. However, all the reported CDs-based TDPCs materials could only be activated by ultraviolet (UV) light, which may narrow their application ranges. Compared to UV light, visible light as an excitation source has many advantages, such as less phototoxicity and deeper penetration [21,22]. Therefore, it is of interest to obtain visible-light-activated CDs-based TDPCs materials.

The formation of multiple RTP emission centers needs CDs’ engineering and the ingenious selection of matrix. Adding C=O/C=N onto the surface of CDs to enhance spin–orbit coupling for producing triplet state, and doping heteroatoms, such as N, to facilitate ISC could be the efficient approaches to form multiple centers on CDs [23,24]. Moreover, the ingenious selection of matrix is significantly important to activate multiple emission centers of CDs because it not only confines the motion of the groups and stabilizes the lowest triplet state, but also restrains nonradiative transitions of CDs [25,26]. Additionally, if matrix itself can emit RTP, it may also act as another emissive center.

Noncovalent interactions, including hydrogen bonds and π−π stacking are the main interaction between CDs and matrix. However, most of the reports about CDs-based RTP materials focused on hydrogen bonds providing rigid environment to active the RTP emission centers [27,28]. π−π stacking interactions refer to the interactions between aromatic rings containing π orbitals. As reported in many organic RTP materials, effective π-π stacking between chromophores and matrix with aromatic moieties can stabilize triplet excitons and reduce nonradiative relaxation, resulting in efficient RTP [29–34]. However, to our knowledge, less studies were performed to investigate the effect of π-π stacking on the RTP of CDs and use such effect to construct CDs-based TDPCs materials to date.

In this work, CDs were synthesized by hydrothermal treatment of levofloxacin and ethanolamine (LEE-CDs). The selection of levofloxacin as a precursor was expected to preserve its RTP emissive center and form plenty of aromatic moieties, given it is a molecule of fluorinated quinolone derivative with RTP property [19,35]. And ethanolamine was applied for further forming C=O/C=N groups and doping N in LEE-CDs. Melamine (MA) and cyanuric acid (CA) with large π systems, and urea and biuret without aromatic moieties were selected as matrices, respectively. LEE-CDs were then encapsulated into the aromatic or non-aromatic matrices to form the composites named as LEE-CDs@MA, LEE-CDs@CA, LEE-CDs@Urea and LEE-CDs@Biuret, respectively. Interestingly, LEE-CDs@MA and LEE-CDs@CA exhibited visible-light-excited TDPCs varying from red to yellow green, yet LEE-CDs@Urea and LEE-CDs@Biuret could not exhibit TDPCs.

Melamine (MA) was obtained from Sangon BIotech Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Levofloxacin, Urea and Biuret were purchased from Shanghai Macklin BIochemical Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Ethanolamine was received from Tian in Fuyu Fine Chemical Co., Ltd (Tianjin, China). All chemicals were used without any further purification. The ultrapure water used in all experiments was purified by the Water Purifier Nanopure water system (18.3 MΩ cm).

A Hitachi F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Tokyo, Japan) was employed to record the steady-state fluorescence and phosphorescence spectra (solution/powder). In addition, a FLS1000 fluorescence spectrometer (Edinburgh, England) was used to identify the fluorescence lifetime and absolute phosphorescence quantum yield of LEE-CDs and LEE-CDs@MA. The time-resolved phosphorescence spectra were collected at room temperature by FLS-1000 (Edinburgh). The X-ray diffraction (XRD) profiles was collected using a D8 ADVANCE X-ray diffractometer (Bruker AXS, German) with Cu-Kα radiation (40 kV, 40 mA, λ = 1.5418 Å) at a scanning rate of 1° min−1 in the range from 10° to 70°. The Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were recorded using a FT-IR200 spectrometer (Thermo America group, Miami, FL) with an ATR technique over the range 600–3900 cm−1. The morphology and size were analyzed using transmission electron microscopy (TEM; Hitachi H-7000, operated at 80 kV). UV–vis absorption spectra were scanned by using of a U-3900 UV–vis spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan). Meanwhile, scanning electron microscope (SEM) images were recorded by JSM-IT500 instrument.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and purification processes of LEE-CDs, LEE-CDs@MA, LEE-CDs@CA, LEE-CDs@Urea, LEE-CDs@Biuret
ce:section.ce:para: Synthesis of LEE-CDs: The LEE-CDs were synthesized by a simple hydrothermal method. Briefly, 0.1 g levofloxacin, 0.3 mL ethanolamine and 10 mL ultrapure water were mixed together. After 5 min of ultrasonic treatment, the formed mixture was transferred into a 50 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 7 h. Then it was allowed to cool down to room temperature. A yellow solution was collected after the product was centrifugated at 10 000 rpm for 5 min. Then the solution was diluted to its absorbance of 1.5 for further use.

Synthesis of LEE-CDs@MA: Firstly, 0.9 g of Melamine (MA) was dispersed with 12.5 mL ultrapure water, and then 4.5 mL of the LEE-CDs solution was mixed with the suspension. After the mixture was transferred into a 50 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 200 °C for 7 h. The solid product was collected by centrifuging the suspension at 10 000 rpm for 5 min at room temperature and washed with ultrapure water for two times. Finally, after it was dried at 60 °C overnight, the solid was ground and the powder was collected for characterization.
The synthesis of LEE-CDs@CA was similar to the synthesis processes of LEE-CDs@MA, in which 0.9 g MA was replaced by 0.9 g CA.

Synthesis of LEE-CDs@Urea: Firstly, 2.5 mL of LEE-CDs and 1.8 g of Urea were dissolved with 3.5 mL DI water in a beaker by ultrasonic oscillation. The mixture was heated in an oven at 200 °C for 4 h with foil covering for slowing down the water evaporation. After it was allowed to cool down to room temperature naturally, the final products were obtained by grinding the solids into powder.
The synthesis of LEE-CDs@Biuret was similar to the synthesized processes of LEE-CDs@Urea, in which 1.8 g urea was replaced by the 1.8 g biuret.

The matrices of MA, CA, urea, and biuret were also treated by the similar processes to the preparation of LEE-CDs@MA, LEE-CDs@CA, LEE-CDs@Urea and LEE-CDs@Biuret, respectively, without LEE-CDs being added.
The “butterfly” and “rabbit” pattern were printed on the sticky papers, and then the LEE-CDs@MA powder was evenly smeared on the paper.

Characterized by TEM, LEE-CDs in Fig. 1 A exhibited a typical morphology of common carbon dots, that is, monodispersed spherical nanoparticles with an average size of about 8.7 nm. Moreover, the HR-TEM image revealed a typical lattice spacing of 0.23 nm, which fitted well with the (100) facet of graphite [17]. And the Raman spectrum of LEE-CDs, in which a G band at 1585 cm−1 and a D band at 1342 cm−1 corresponded to the graphitic sp2 carbon structures and disordered sp3 carbon structures (Fig. S1) [36]. The FT-IR spectrum of LEE-CDs (Fig. 1B) displayed a broad absorption band containing two tiny peaks at around 3353 to 3184 cm−1 assigned to the stretching vibration of –OH and –NH2 bonds, which could provide sites to form hydrogen bonds with N or O related groups in the matrices [37]. The absorption at 1615 cm−1 was indicative of the existence of C=O/C=N on the surface of LEE-CDs, which might enhance spin–orbit coupling to produce triplet excited states [38]. The bands at 1574 and1475 cm−1 were attributed to the skeletal vibrations of the aromatic rings on the surface of LEE-CDs, which could potentially suffer from the π - π stacking interaction with 1, 3, 5-triazinane in MA and CA [14,39]. The UV–vis spectrum of LEE-CDs dispersed in aqueous solution (Fig. 1C) showed two main absorption peaks. The stronger one peaked at 289 nm originated from the π-π* transition of large aromatic sp2 domain, indicating that the nitrogen heterocycle in levofloxacin formed extended π conjugations with ethanolamine in LEE-CDs [40,41]. The other one at 332 nm was due to the transition of a non-bonding (lone pair) electron on C=O/C=N groups connected to large π conjugation up to a π* antibonding molecular orbital (n-π*) [42,43]. As can be seen from Fig. 1D, the fluorescence spectra of LEE-CDs in aqueous solution were independent of excitation wavelengths in the UV region (340–400 nm), while the fluorescence emission was red-shifted with excitation wavelength in the visible light region (400 ∼ 480 nm). It indicates there were multiple fluorescence emissive centers on LEE-CDs which could be activated by UV and visible lights. And the maximum emission was centered at 477 nm under excitation at 420 nm with an absolute FL QY of 5.3 % and an average lifetime (τ) of 5.56 ns (Table S1). However, since LEE-CDs could not be dried to a solid, but to a viscous material, no solid fluorescence and RTP were exhibited.

The selected matrices could generate green (Urea, Biuret and MA) and blue green (CA) RTP emissions (Fig. S2) with an excitation-dependent characteristic (Figs. S3A–D) under UV excitation, and their maximum emissions were located around at 480, 492, 496 and 468 nm, respectively (Figs. S3A–D). However, excited by visible lights, urea and biuret displayed very weak yellow RTP emissions, while no RTP emissions were observed for MA and CA (Fig. S2). Moreover, all the four matrices exhibited single-color RTP emissions, that is, the RTP retained a single color with time decaying whatever excited by UV or visible light (Fig. S2). Then, LEE-CDs were incapsulated into the four matrices by heating their mixtures at 200 °C. As shown in Fig. 2 , LEE-CDs were well dispersed with average diameters of 9.2, 8.1, 13.2 and 9.2 nm in the matrices of urea, biuret, MA and CA, respectively. It clearly showed that LEE-CDs encapsulated in MA were larger than the pristine LEE-CDs (8.7 nm, Fig. 1A), suggesting that LEE-CDs were little aggregated due to the confinement of the matrices. The FT-IR spectra of LEE-CDs@Urea, LEE-CDs@Biuret, LEE-CDs@MA and LEE-CDs@CA were very similar to the matrices of urea, biuret, MA and CA, suggesting that LEE-CDs were incapsulated into the matrices (Fig. S4).

Shown in Fig. S5 are the fluorescence emission spectra of LEE-CDs@Urea, LEE-CDs@Biuret, LEE-CDs@MA and LEE-CDs@CA. The fluorescence emission spectra of LEE-CDs@Urea, LEE-CDs@Biuret and LEE-CDs@CA were excitation-independent with the maximum emissions being located at 506, 496 and 470 nm under excitation at the wavelength of 280 nm (Table S2). Apparently, the fluorescence emissions of LEE-CDs@MA were also excitation-independent when excited in the UV region. The maximum emission was located at 484 nm under excitation at 320 nm. Under visible light excitation, the emissions of LEE-CDs@MA were red-shifted with the excitation light moving to longer wavelengths.

LEE-CDs@Urea and LEE-CDs@Biuret displayed green RTP after excited by UV light and yellow RTP after excited by visible lights, yet no TDPCs could be observed from them (Fig. 3 A). The RTP emission spectra of LEE-CDs@Urea and LEE-CDs@Biuret under excitation by UV and visible lights are of slight excitation dependence, as shown in Fig. 3B and C. However, time-resolved phosphorescence emission spectra (TRPES) could clearly identify the excitation dependence of the RTP emissions of LEE-CDs@Urea and LEE-CDs@Biuret. As shown in Fig. 3D and E, under excitation at 400 nm, the maximum RTP emissions of LEE-CDs@urea and CDs@Biuret were located at 548 and 538 nm, originated from the emissive center on LEE-CDs. Under excitation at 360 nm, the RTP spectra of LEE-CDs@Urea and CDs@Biuret peaked at 520 and 510 nm (Fig. 3F and G). Moreover, with time decaying, their RTP intensity decreased gradually at 548 and 538 nm, and quenched sharply with time decaying from 10 to 100 ms at 520 and 510 nm. No wavelength shifting was observed under excitation at 400 and 360 nm. Thus, LEE-CDs@urea and CDs@Biuret could not generate TDPCs under excitation by UV or visible lights.

Interestingly, LEE-CDs@MA emitted green RTP of a single color under UV light excitation, while, exhibited TDPCs varying from red to yellow green under visible light excitation (photos in 4A). Moreover, TDPCs can be observed from LEE-CDs@CA whatever excited by UV or visible light. Visible light activated LEE-CDs@CA to produce TDPCs varying from red, orange, yellow to yellow green with time decaying. UV light activated LEE-CDs@CA to generate TDPCs from yellow, yellow green to green with time decaying (photos in 4B). And the RTP spectra of LEE-CDs@MA in Fig. 4 C clearly showed that, under excitation at the wavelengths less than 380 nm, the material generated single peak emission at 501 nm. When excitation wavelength was longer than 380 nm, a peak at 562 nm became dominant. However, under excitation at wavelengths less than 380 nm, the RTP emission spectra of LEE-CDs@CA have two peaks, a dominant one at 550 nm and a should peak at 485 nm (Fig. 4D). As the excitation light moved to the longer wavelengths (from 400 to 440 nm), the peak at 485 nm gradually disappeared. It can be concluded that there were multi RTP emission centers in LEE-CDs@MA and LEE-CDs@CA, which could be activated by UV and visible lights.

As shown in Fig. S6, the changing trend of RTP in TRPES of LEE-CDs@MA is very consistent with TDPCs displayed in the photos (Fig. 4A). Under excitation at 360, the RTP spectra of LEE-CDs@MA are of single peak at 509 nm, that is, a single color of green RTP after irradiated by UV light and the intensity decreased with time decaying (Fig. S6A). After excited by visible lights, the emissions at short and long wavelengths were both blue shifted and their intensities decreased with time decaying. For example, the emission at 490 nm was shifted to 475 nm, and the emission at 590 nm was shifted to 548 nm by excitation at 400 nm (Fig. S6B), to 575 nm by excitation at 420 nm (Fig. S6C) and to 562 nm by excitation at 440 nm (Fig. S6D). Since the peak at 590 nm was much stronger than that at 490 nm and the latter is quenched sooner, TDPCs from red to yellow green was exhibited after being irradiated by the visible light. To investigate the TDPCs mechanism of LEE-CDs@MA, TRPES were analyzed by Gaussian function fitting. All the TRPES of LEE-CDs@MA at different delay times could be fitted into three Gaussian peaks under excitation at 400 (Fig. 5 ), 420 (Fig. S7) and 440 nm (Fig. S8), respectively. Taking the excitation at 400 nm as an example, the TRPES could be fitted into three simple Gaussian peaks at 488, 550 and 605 nm, respectively (Fig. 5). The emission band at 605 nm occupied the largest fraction, while the peaks at 550 and 488 nm occupied nearly the same fraction in the TRPES (Fig. 5A). Therefore, at the initial stage, the RTP of LEE-CDs@MA was red color. And the lifetimes at 488, 550 and 605 nm are 218.57, 490.30 and 133.58 ms, respectively (Fig. S9 and Table S3), in which, the lifetime of RTP emission at 550 nm was the longest, and the lifetime of RTP at 605 nm is shorter than that at 488 nm. With time decaying, the fractions at 605 and 488 nm were decaying faster than the emission at 550 nm (Fig. 5B and C). At 721 and 900 ms, the fraction at 550 nm left was much larger than the other two parts (Fig. 5D and E). Therefore, TDPCs varied from red, orange, yellow and to yellow green. The emission at 488 nm was consistent with that of MA, indicating this emissive center was of the matrix. The fraction at 550 nm also existed in the RTP spectra of LEE-CDs@Urea and LEE-CDs@Biuret, originating from one emissive center on LEE-CDs confined in the matrices, due to the rigid environment formed by hydrogen bonds. The fraction at 605 nm should be another emissive center on LEE-CDs, which could be activated by MA, not yet by urea and biuret. Urea is the raw material of MA and Biuret, and the distinguishable structure difference between MA with urea and biuret is the triazine ring, which could form the π−π stacking interaction with the aromatic rings on the surface of LEE-CDs. Thus, it can be inferred that the emission center was activated by the π−π stacking combined with the hydrogen bonds formed between the matrix and LEE-CDs.

Moreover, TRPES of LEE-CDs@CA exhibited two peaks at 525 and 605 nm after being excited by the 365 nm light (Fig. S10A). With time decaying, the peak at 605 nm gradually disappeared. At the decaying time of 306 ms, TRPES of LEE-CDs@MA became a single peak spectrum centered at 525 nm. Under excitation at 400 and 420 nm, the peak at long wavelength gradually blue shifted, while the peak at short wavelength gradually disappeared (Figs. S10B and C). Finally, the TRPES of LEE-CDs@CA became a single-color emission centered at about 550 nm (a yellow color). Further, three fractions at 601, 540 and 500 nm were obtained by deconvolving the TRPES of LEE-CDs@CA under excitation at 400 (Fig. 6 ) and 420 (Fig. S11). The fraction at 601 nm occupied the largest part of the TRPES in the initial stage at the decaying time of 10 ms and the fraction at 540 nm occupied the smallest part of the TRPES of LEE-CDs@CA (Fig. 6A). And the lifetimes of the fractions at 500, 540 and 601 nm were 280.32, 355.84 and 322.39 ms, respectively (Fig. S12 and Table S3). With time decaying, the fraction at 500 nm decreased very soon, and nearly disappeared at the decaying time of about 200 ms. Also, the fraction at 601 nm decayed sooner than the fraction at 540 nm because of the short lifetime. From 500 to 900 ms, the occupation of the fraction at 540 nm became larger than that at 601 nm. Accordingly, eye detectable TDPCs from red, orange to yellow were displayed by LEE-CDs@CA excited by visible light. Under UV light excitation, the TRPES of LEE-CDs@CA could be deconvolved into three fractions at 605, 556 and 520 nm (Fig. S13). Interestingly, the lifetimes of the fractions at 520, 556 and 605 nm were 513.43, 219.57 and 372.75 ms (Fig. S14), the lifetime of the fraction at 520 nm was much longer than those at 556 and 605 nm. The fractions at 520 and 605 nm were the main parts, and the fraction at 556 nm nearly can be ignored in the initial stage at the decaying time of 10 ms, thus yellow was displayed first (Fig. S13A). With time decaying, the fractions at 605 and 520 nm decreased, and the fraction at 556 nm was obviously displayed, but the fraction at 520 nm became the main part. (Figs. S13B–D). Therefore, the RTP colors varied from yellow, yellow green to green.

The mechanism of TDPCs for LEE-CDs@MA and LEE-CDs@CA is schematically illustrated in Fig. 7 . As for LEE-CDs@Urea and LEE-CDs@Biuret, hydrogen bonds were formed between LEE-CDs and the two matrices, and between the individual matrices, resulting in the rigid environment for the protection of the triplet states in urea and biuret (TM) and the N-related triplet state (TN) in LEE-CDs [19]. However, the hydrogen bonds didn't have the enough strength to activate the O-related triplet state (TO) in LEE-CDs [19].After excited by UV light, the excitons on the S1 state of the matrix could transfer to TM, then produced green RTP by the radiative transition from TM to S0. However, the excitons on the S1 state could not transfer to TN of LEE-CDs. Therefore, under UV light excitation, only green RTP could be generated by LEE-CDs@Urea and LEE-CDs@Biuret. On the other hand, excited by visible light, the excitons on S1’ state of LEE-CDs could transfer to TN, but not to TM, which resulted in single-color yellow RTP. Therefore, no TDPCS occurred for LEE-CDs@Urea and LEE-CDs@Biuret under excitation by UV or visible light.

As for LEE-CDs@MA, there were lots of hydrogen bonds formed between LEE-CDs and the matrix, and between the individual matrices. Like LEE-CDs@Urea and LEE-CDs@Biuret, hydrogen bonds activated the triplet states of both TM in MA and TN in LEE-CDs. Moreover, combined with hydrogen bonds, π−π stacking interaction between the aromatic moieties of LEE-CDs and MA could activate TO in LEE-CDs. Under UV light excitation, the excitons on S1 state of MA only transferred to TM, not to TN. As a result, RTP was released with only one single green color. Moreover, excited by visible light, the excitons on S1’ state of LEE-CDs could transfer not only to TM and TN, but also to TO. Thus, RTP from LEE-CDs@MA contained three fractions, green, yellow and red. Due to their intensity and lifetime were different, LEE-CDs@MA exhibited TDPCs varying from red to yellow green.

The main structural difference between CA and MA is that the three –NH2 groups are substituted by three –OH groups, resulting in unique TDPCs for LEE-CDs@CA initiated by both UV and visible lights. Due to the stronger ability of –OH to attract electrons from aromatic moieties, the π−π interaction between LEE-CDs and CA became more intensive. Thus, TM, TN and TO were all activated, and the excitons on S1 and S1’ states could individually transfer to all the triplet states, TM, TN and TO. Therefore, whatever excited by UV or visible light, the RTP from LEE-CDs@CA contained green, yellow and red three fractions. Due to their intensity and lifetime were different, LEE-CDs@CA exhibited TDPCs varying from red to yellow green for visible light excitation, and yellow to green for UV light excitation.

Considering its good TDPCs performance, LEE-CDs@MA, as a representative, was applied in dynamic anti-counterfeiting. As shown in Fig. 8 , after turning off the 365 nm UV lamp, green butterflies and rabbits were displayed and lasted for at least 2 s. After being excited by the visible light, a red orange butterfly and rabbit appeared, then became yellow and finally turned into yellow green ones. These phenomena could be easily recorded by a mobile phone, and the patterns expressed by this unique TDPCs property are hard to mimic or fake.

The N-related triplet state and the O-related triplet state can be formed by adding C=O/C=N groups and doping N and O in LEE-CDs synthesized with levofloxacin and ethanolamine as precursors. Beside the individual RTP emissive center (TM) in the matrices, there are three potential emission centers in LEE-CDs@Urea, LEE-CDs@Biuret, LEE-CDs@MA and LEE-CDs@CA. Only hydrogen bonds existing between LEE-CDs and matrix can activate TN and TM emissive centers, such as LEE-CDs@Urea and LEE-CDs@Biuret. However, due to the short Stokes shift between the TN and TM centers, TDPCs cannot be produced in these CDs based materials. When the matrices have aromatic moieties, the combination of π−π stacking interaction between the aromatic moieties of LEE-CDs and the matrices (MA and CA) with hydrogen bonds can activate the mute TO in LEE-CDs. The dynamic ratio of the RTP emissive bands changing over time from the three centers (TM, TN and TO) result in TDPCs. LEE-CDs@MA and LEE-CDs@CA are the more intelligent anti-counterfeiting materials compared to those with a single color RTP, and the ideal candidates for high level anti-counterfeiting.
There are no conflicts to declare.

Mei Wang: Writing – original draft, Data curation. Xiaobo Sun: Writing – review & editing, Conceptualization. Wei Pan: Software, Resources. Xiuxia Wang: Project administration, Investigation. Jinping Wang: Supervision, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare the following financial interests/personal relationships which may be considered as potential competing interests.

Jinping Wang reports financial support was provided by National Natural Science Foundation of China. Jinping Wang reports financial support was provided by National Key Research and Development Program of China. If there are other authors, they declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by National Key R&D Program of China (2017YFE0105200) and National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 31572181).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.optmat.2024.115856.

ce:def-description.ce:para: levofloxacin and ethanolamine were applied to synthesize CDs
